summary: Ronacher argues that developers often choose the GPL by default without understanding its implications, and he suggests this is a mistake. He contrasts GPL’s copyleft philosophy with permissive licenses like BSD/MIT, emphasizing how the GPL’s requirements can block interoperability and constrain downstream use. He explains that GPL permits private internal use without sharing changes, but forbids combining GPL code with non-GPL code, which creates practical friction. He frames “freedom” differently for GPL versus BSD, noting GPL’s one-way permanence and its ideological roots. He concludes that licensing is a strategic choice with real trade-offs, and developers should decide deliberately rather than follow the crowd.
key_points: GPL and BSD embody opposing philosophies: copyleft guarantees reciprocity, while permissive licenses grant broad rights with minimal restrictions.
GPL’s compatibility limits can block use of important libraries (e.g., readline), degrading user experience for non-GPL projects.
GPL allows private modification without disclosure unless distribution occurs, so companies can benefit without sharing changes.
Permissive licenses enable relicensing and commercial reuse, but make monetization harder without strong product/community support.
License choice is irreversible in practice once GPL is released, so the decision demands forethought.
money_quotes: I suppose many people acted like me and chose the GPL because everyone else did but didn't know about all the implications it has.
BSD restricts the rights, GPL permits.
Like for the BSD license someone can take the application, rebrand it and sell it, however the license demands that the modified source is available under the same license.
But what this also means is that non GPL code can't use GPL code which is also the main problem of it.
That kind of freedom however is a one-way ticket.
Before you license your code under an Open Source license: Think about the license!
Many just do because they haven't read the license either.
key_insight: Licensing is a strategic, irreversible choice with concrete technical and business consequences, so developers should choose deliberately rather than default to the GPL.
